protocols.title: Rapid Disease Outbreak
protocols.description: Disease spreading quickly across multiple trees or a large portion of your farm
immediate_actions: STOP harvesting from visibly infected trees immediately
Do not move harvested pods from infected areas to healthy areas
Take photos of affected trees/pods for documentation
Contact your local COCOBOD extension officer today
short_term_actions: Remove and destroy all visibly infected pods and plant material
Create a buffer zone — avoid working from infected areas to healthy areas without changing clothes
Do not share tools between infected and healthy sections without cleaning
Apply copper-based fungicide to healthy trees bordering the infected area as protection

contact.when: Same day — this is an emergency
contact.what_to_say: Tell them: the disease name (if known), how many trees are affected, how fast it is spreading, and your farm location

protocols.title: Suspected CSSVD Infection
protocols.description: Signs of Cocoa Swollen Shoot Virus Disease — there is no cure, but rapid response limits spread
immediate_actions: Do NOT remove any trees yourself — wait for COCOBOD confirmation
Isolate the suspected area — do not allow people or equipment in and out
Document all affected trees with photos and a sketch of their location
Report to COCOBOD extension officer within 24 hours
short_term_actions: COCOBOD will confirm diagnosis and authorize tree removal
You may be eligible for compensation under the CSSVD eradication program
Do not sell planting material from affected areas
Plan for replanting with CSSVD-tolerant varieties after official guidance

contact.who: COCOBOD Extension Officer — CSSVD is a notifiable disease
contact.what_to_say: Describe the symptoms — swollen shoots, red leaf banding, distorted pods — and your exact farm location

protocols.title: Pesticide / Chemical Exposure
protocols.description: Accidental exposure to agricultural chemicals — pesticides, fungicides, herbicides
immediate_actions: Move away from the source of exposure immediately
If skin/eyes exposed: flush with large amounts of clean water for 15–20 minutes
If inhaled: move to fresh air, loosen tight clothing
If swallowed: do NOT induce vomiting — call for medical help immediately
Remove contaminated clothing while flushing skin
short_term_actions: Seek medical attention at the nearest health facility
Bring the pesticide container or label to the health facility so doctors know what chemical was involved
If symptoms are severe (difficulty breathing, seizures, loss of consciousness) — treat as a medical emergency

contact.what_to_say: State the chemical involved if known, route of exposure (skin, eyes, ingestion, inhalation), and symptoms

protocols.title: Risk of Total Crop Failure
protocols.description: Situation where large proportion of current season's harvest is at risk
immediate_actions: Prioritize harvesting any pods that are still healthy and close to ripe
Begin aggressive removal of all infected material immediately
Document the extent of damage (photos, count of affected trees)
Contact COCOBOD extension officer for emergency farm visit
short_term_actions: Apply fungicide spray to all remaining healthy trees as protection
Increase harvest frequency to every 5–7 days to catch pods before disease spreads
Ask COCOBOD about emergency input support programs
Avoid replanting in severely affected soil without consultation

contact.who: COCOBOD Extension Officer + District Agricultural Officer
contact.what_to_say: Report the scale of loss, disease type, and request emergency farm visit and input support

general_emergency_contacts.note: Contact your local COCOBOD District Office for the correct phone numbers in your area. Numbers vary by district.
resources: Your local COCOBOD District Office (ask at the nearest buying center for contact)
Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MoFA) local office
